content.text:
Lets pick these up - 
#801 (mobile overlay nav), #802 (hierarchical asset browse), #803 (asset health split), #804 (Marquez in harness), #805 (lineage graph view)
Let me pull up these five issues to understand what we're building.

result.output:
===== #801 =====
{"body":"User-reported (follow-up to the #617/#688 responsive pass): on mobile, expanding the hamburger/Sider squeezes the page content because the Sider participates in the flex layout. It should render as an **overlay drawer** (AntD `Drawer` or Sider with `breakpoint` + fixed positioning + scrim) above the content at small viewports; desktop behaviour unchanged.

**Acceptance criteria**
- [ ] ≤ md viewports: expanded nav overlays content (scrim behind, content width unchanged)
- [ ] Desktop unchanged
- [ ] Playwright/component test pins the overlay behaviour at 390px","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOSBITx88AAAACfH8OBQ","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"milestone":{"number":4,"title":"v1.1 Backlog","description":"Un-scheduled post-v1 backlog for the v1.1 cycle (renamed 2026-07-04 from 'Backlog (post-v1 / testing)'). Issues here are themed in context/post-v1-roadmap.md; when scheduled they move to a v1.1 Week N milestone. New post-v1 issues land here by default.","dueOn":null},"number":801,"state":"OPEN","title":"Mobile: sidebar/hamburger menu should overlay the page, not consume layout width"}

===== #802 =====
{"body":"User-reported (follow-up to the G-d asset views #760/#773): the current Assets page is a single flat table with one row per object. Wanted instead:

1. Assets grouped **under their connection** (as a separate view, not inside the existing connection tab).
2. **Drill-down hierarchy**: connections list → click a connection → its databases/catalogs → click → schemas/namespaces → click → tables/paths (the asset leaves). Asset identity stays the ADR 0034 OpenLineage namespace+name — this is a presentation regrouping, the namespace already encodes the connection/db/schema segments.

**Acceptance criteria**
- [ ] Tree/drill-down browse rooted at connections; leaves open the existing AssetDetail
- [ ] Env grouping preserved (DEV/QA distinct assets, grouped visually per ADR 0034)
- [ ] Flat table retained as a secondary 'all assets' list/search view (or explicitly dropped with user sign-off)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOSBITx88AAAACfH8OBQ","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"milestone":{"number":4,"title":"v1.1 Backlog","description":"Un-scheduled post-v1 backlog for the v1.1 cycle (renamed 2026-07-04 from 'Backlog (post-v1 / testing)'). Issues here are themed in context/post-v1-roadmap.md; when scheduled they move to a v1.1 Week N milestone. New post-v1 issues land here by default.","dueOn":null},"number":802,"state":"OPEN","title":"Assets: hierarchical browse under Connections (connection → database → schema → table), not a flat table"}

===== #803 =====
{"body":"User-reported: the asset Health signal conflates two different things. Split into:
- **Asset connection health** — can DataQ currently reach the datasource backing this asset (connection test status / last successful run against it / operational errors).
- **Suite health** — the ADR 0005 severity-weighted score of the suites attached to this asset (the existing signal).

**Acceptance criteria**
- [ ] Two clearly-labelled health indicators on AssetDetail (and the assets list lead strip where applicable)
- [ ] Connection-health derivation documented (no new polling loop without design note)
- [ ] Operational `error`/`skip` results feed connection health, not suite health (matches #122 semantics)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOSBITx88AAAACfH8OBQ","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"milestone":{"number":4,"title":"v1.1 Backlog","description":"Un-scheduled post-v1 backlog for the v1.1 cycle (renamed 2026-07-04 from 'Backlog (post-v1 / testing)'). Issues here are themed in context/post-v1-roadmap.md; when scheduled they move to a v1.1 Week N milestone. New post-v1 issues land here by default.","dueOn":null},"number":803,"state":"OPEN","title":"AssetDetail: split Health into connection health vs suite-results health"}

===== #804 =====
{"body":"User-reported: in prod none of the assets have lineage details. The #762 `LineageProvider` seam + Marquez reference impl and the dev-only compose profile exist, but no Marquez runs against the deployed app and the OL emitter is dark-by-default (ADR 0034), so `lineage_edges` only fills from dbt manifests (#759) where configured.

**Scope (harness-side, ADR 0021 — tracked here since the harness repo is untracked)**
- Add a Marquez service to the harness (or a small ACA app while the Azure window lasts; local compose after wind-down).
- Enable OL emission (+ dbt lineage refresh) for the harness flows so Flow A/B/C/D assets get real upstream/downstream edges.
- Verify AssetDetail lineage renders end-to-end against it.

**Acceptance criteria**
- [ ] Marquez reachable from the app (env-configured, off by default)
- [ ] At least the Flow-A chain shows ≥2 lineage edges in the UI
- [ ] Runbook section in the harness README / wake-window script flag","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOSBITx88AAAACqJ9Nfg","name":"ops","description":"Operational / environment / infra lifecycle task (not app code)","color":"D4C5F9"}],"milestone":{"number":4,"title":"v1.1 Backlog","description":"Un-scheduled post-v1 backlog for the v1.1 cycle (renamed 2026-07-04 from 'Backlog (post-v1 / testing)'). Issues here are themed in context/post-v1-roadmap.md; when scheduled they move to a v1.1 Week N milestone. New post-v1 issues land here by default.","dueOn":null},"number":804,"state":"OPEN","title":"Harness: stand up Marquez + backfill lineage so prod assets show lineage"}

===== #805 =====
{"body":"User-reported (follow-up to #760/#761): AssetDetail renders upstream and downstream as two separate list boxes. Replace with a single **left-to-right graph** — upstream nodes on the left, the current asset centered, downstream on the right; nodes clickable (navigate to that asset), depth ≥2 via the existing blast-radius BFS.

Implementation note: prefer a light dependency (e.g. reactflow) — check the ADR 0031 license guardrail (MIT ok) and lazy-load like recharts (ADR 0022).

**Acceptance criteria**
- [ ] Graph view with directional layout + clickable nodes
- [ ] Handles 0-edge assets gracefully (current empty-state preserved)
- [ ] Mobile: horizontal scroll inside the card, no page overflow
- [ ] Dependency license checked (no strong-copyleft, CONTRIBUTING rule 40)","labels":[{"id":"LA_kwDOSBITx88AAAACfH8OBQ","name":"enhancement","description":"New feature or request","color":"a2eeef"}],"milestone":{"number":4,"title":"v1.1 Backlog","description":"Un-scheduled post-v1 backlog for the v1.1 cycle (renamed 2026-07-04 from 'Backlog (post-v1 / testing)'). New post-v1 issues land here by default.","dueOn":null},"number":805,"state":"OPEN","title":"Lineage: graph view (upstream LHS → asset → downstream RHS) instead of separate boxes"}

=== provider.py head ===
"""The `LineageProvider` seam — pull a lineage graph from a catalog (ADR 0034, #762).

Mirrors `orchestration.base.OrchestrationProvider`: one provider-agnostic interface
that speaks normalized DTOs, so service code routes by the configured provider and
never branches on the concrete catalog. **Marquez is the reference implementation**
(`lineage.marquez`); the same seam is the extension point for catalogs deferred by
ADR 0034 — **do not build these here**:

- **DataHub** — same seam, deferred until a user brings one (8 GB-RAM Kafka/OpenSearch
  footprint disqualifies it from the reference compose stack; our #758 emitter already
  feeds its native OpenLineage receiver).
- **OpenMetadata** — must **not** integrate via its `openmetadata-ingestion` SDK
  (Collate source-available, non-compete — prohibited by ADR 0031); a REST-only
  integration would need its own ADR.
- **Microsoft Purview** — parked (Atlas API, preview-grade OpenLineage, Azure-only —
  contra the wind-down posture).

**The node-kind contract (the #762 comment).** A pulled graph's downstream nodes are
**not always tables**: a governance catalog that ingests Power BI lineage surfaces a
*report* sitting downstream of a monitored dataset, and DataHub/Purview surface *jobs*
and *dashboards*. So every node carries a :class:`LineageNodeKind`, not an implicit
"this is a dataset" assumption. Today only ``DATASET`` nodes have an OpenLineage
identity that maps to a DataQ `assets` row (and thus to a `lineage_edges` edge);
``JOB`` nodes are collapsed through, and every other kind (BI report, dashboard, an
unknown a newer catalog emits) **parses without crashing** and is carried on the graph
so a future capable provider round-trips it into the asset model with no seam change.

**Pure + read-only.** `get_lineage` performs one catalog read and returns a
:class:`LineageGraph`; it never writes. Persisting the pulled edges into the
`lineage_edges` cache is a separate concern (`lineage.pull.refresh_pulled_edges`), the
same split as `orchestration.base` (parse) vs `orchestration_service` (persist).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import StrEnum
from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

content.text:
I've now mapped all five. Here's what I found and my plan before I start cutting PRs.

**The four frontend issues are all actionable now** (App shell, Assets page, AssetDetail, health logic + their existing tests are all in place):
- **#801** — Sider currently sits in the flex layout (`collapsedWidth={0}`), so expanding it on mobile squeezes content. Fix: overlay drawer + scrim at ≤`lg`, desktop unchanged.
- **#802** — Assets is a flat table; rebuild as connection → database → schema → table drill-down (the namespace already encodes those segments), keep flat table as a secondary search view.
- **#803** — split the single health signal into *connection health* (operational error/skip → reachability) vs *suite health* (severity score). Small backend touch to expose the reachability axis cleanly.
- **#805** — replace the two upstream/downstream list boxes with a left→right lineage graph; needs a lazy-loaded MIT graph lib (reactflow/`@xyflow/react`), license-checked per rule 40.

**#804 is the problem child.** It's harness/ops (untracked repo, ADR 0021) and its acceptance criteria ("Marquez reachable from the app", "Flow-A chain shows ≥2 edges *in the UI*") assume a running deployed app — but per memory the **Azure subscription was disabled today (2026-07-12)**, so prod is down and there's no ACA to attach Marquez to. The compose `lineage` profile + `LineageProvider`/Marquez seam already exist; what's missing is a live catalog wired to a reachable app.

Two things are genuinely your call before I execute:

questions.question: Given prod is down (Azure disabled today), how should I handle #804 (stand up Marquez + backfill lineage)?

options.label: Local-compose verify + defer prod
options.description: Bring up the existing docker-compose `lineage` profile locally, wire LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez, verify AssetDetail renders ≥2 Flow-A edges against the LOCAL stack, write the harness runbook note, and leave the prod-against-Marquez validation deferred until infra returns. Closes most of the issue now.

options.label: Defer #804 entirely
options.description: Skip it this pass — it's blocked on live infra. Comment the Azure-disabled blocker on the issue and pick it back up when prod/a new host is available. Focus the session on the four frontend PRs.

options.label: App-side config only
options.description: Do only the in-repo app-side pieces (config docs, any provider wiring gaps) as a small PR, and leave the harness-side Marquez stand-up + UI verification for when infra returns.

questions.question: How do you want the four frontend issues delivered?

options.label: One PR per issue (Recommended)
options.description: Four separate branches/PRs (#801, #802, #803, #805), each with tests + docs + /code-review, per the one-functionality-per-PR working agreement. Slower but clean history and reviewable.

options.label: One stacked chain
options.description: Sequential stacked PRs off each other (they touch overlapping asset files). Faster to build but reviews are coupled.

options.label: Single combined branch
options.description: All four on one branch/PR. Fastest, but violates the one-functionality-per-commit rule and makes review harder.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "Given prod is down (Azure disabled today), how should I handle #804 (stand up Marquez + backfill lineage)?"="Local-compose verify + defer prod", "How do you want the four frontend issues delivered?"="One PR per issue (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text: Decisions locked: **#804 → local-compose verify + defer prod**; **frontend → one PR per issue**. Let me set up tracking and start with #801 (cleanest, fully independent).

<Layout>
          <Sider
            width={SHELL.siderWidth}
            theme="light"
            breakpoint="lg"
            collapsedWidth={0}

            collapsed={navCollapsed}
            onBreakpoint={(broken) => {

            }}

              so the footer stays put. */}

                mode="inline"
                selectedKeys={selectedKeys}
                items={NAV_ITEMS}

                  paddingTop: 8,
                  flex: 1,
                  minHeight: 0,
                  overflowY: 'auto',
                }}
              />

                mode="inline"
                selectedKeys={selectedKeys}
                items={footerItems}

input.new_string:
<Layout>
          <Sider
            width={SHELL.siderWidth}
            theme="light"
            breakpoint="lg"
            collapsedWidth={0}
            trigger={null}
            collapsed={narrow}
            onBreakpoint={(broken) => {
              setNarrow(broken);
              // Reset the overlay on any breakpoint crossing so a drawer left open
              // on mobile doesn't re-appear the next time we drop below `lg`.
              setDrawerOpen(false);
            }}
            style={{ borderInlineEnd: `1px solid ${BRAND.border}`, height: '100%' }}
          >
            {/* Desktop: the Sider *is* the nav. Narrow: it collapses to zero width
                and the nav lives in the overlay Drawer below, so it never squeezes
                the content (#801). Rendering the nav only when !narrow also keeps a
                single copy of each nav item in the DOM. */}
            {!narrow && (
              <SideNav selectedKeys={selectedKeys} footerItems={footerItems} onNavigate={noop} />
            )}
          </Sider>
          {/* Narrow-viewport overlay nav: a left Drawer with a scrim (AntD's default
              mask). It floats above the content — the page keeps its full width
              whether the nav is open or shut. Only mounted below `lg`; on desktop
              the Sider handles everything. */}
          {narrow && (
            <Drawer
              id="app-nav-drawer"
              placement="left"
              open={drawerOpen}
              onClose={() => setDrawerOpen(false)}
              width={SHELL.siderWidth}
              styles={{
                body: { padding: 0 },
                header: { borderBottom: `1px solid ${BRAND.border}` },
              }}
              title={
                <Flex align="center" gap={8}>
                  <BrandMark size={20} />
                  <Typography.Text strong style={{ color: BRAND.ink }}>
                    DataQ
                  </Typography.Text>
                </Flex>
              }
            >
              <SideNav
                selectedKeys={selectedKeys}
                footerItems={footerItems}
                onNavigate={() => setDrawerOpen(false)}
              />
            </Drawer>
          )}

result.output: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`high effort → 8 inline angles → dedup (no verify) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** in sequence yourself, in THIS context — do NOT spawn subagents for them. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates are the dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Dedup only (no verify)

Pool all candidates. Dedup near-duplicates only (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). Do NOT run verifiers; do NOT re-judge. Sort by severity.

## Output

Target **at least 5 findings**. If fewer genuine findings exist, emit what you have — do not invent to hit the floor.

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.

1	"""Resolve a connection + suite target to an OpenLineage-shaped asset identity.
2	
3	ADR 0034 adopts the OpenLineage dataset naming spec (``namespace`` + ``name``)
4	verbatim as the canonical asset key, so DataQ's identifiers match
5	``openlineage-dbt``/Spark emissions byte-for-byte — a join, not a mapping
6	layer, when lineage emission/pull lands (#758/#762). This module is the pure
7	resolver: it never touches the network or a store, mirroring
8	``run_target.resolve_target``'s shape (typed, small pure helpers).
9	
10	Per-datasource identity (see docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md
11	§1 and ADR 0034):
12	
13	    snowflake      → snowflake://{normalized account}
14	                     DB.SCHEMA.TABLE (upper unless quoted)
15	    unity_catalog  → unitycatalog://{workspace host[:port]}
16	                     catalog.schema.table (lower unless quoted)
17	    adls_gen2      → abfss://{container}@{account}.dfs.core.windows.net
18	                     {path or pattern base dir}
19	    s3             → s3://{bucket}
20	                     {path or pattern base dir}
21	    iceberg        → {catalog_uri verbatim, or "file"}
22	                     {namespace.table verbatim}
23	
24	Snowflake/UC identifiers fold to the engine's *unquoted* case (Snowflake
25	upper, UC lower) unless a part is double-quote/backtick wrapped, in which
26	case the quotes are stripped and the inner case kept verbatim — the
27	"engine-returned case" the OL clients replicate. Iceberg identifiers are
28	case-sensitive as stored, so no folding is applied there.
29	
30	Orchestration connection types (adf/airflow/dbt) have no asset identity —
31	they are never suite datasources (CLAUDE.md §4) — and raise ``ValueError``;
32	callers (the suite-save hook) wrap this fail-soft rather than surfacing it
33	to a caller who didn't ask for one.
34	"""
35	
36	from __future__ import annotations
37	
38	import re
39	from dataclasses import dataclass
40	from typing import Any
41	from urllib.parse import urlparse
42	
43	# `pattern` is a **regex** (flatfile.py `re.compile`s it, first capture group =
44	# batch key), not a glob — so the asset's directory prefix is the literal text
45	# before the first regex metacharacter.
46	_REGEX_METACHARS = re.compile(r"[\\.^$*+?{}\[\]|()]")
47	
48	
49	@dataclass(frozen=True)
50	class AssetIdentity:
51	    """The OpenLineage ``namespace`` + ``name`` pair that keys an asset row."""
52	
53	    namespace: str
54	    name: str
55	
56	
57	def resolve_asset_identity(
58	    conn_type: str, config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]
59	) -> AssetIdentity:
60	    """Resolve a connection's ``config`` + a suite's ``target`` to an `AssetIdentity`.
61	
62	    Raises `ValueError` if ``conn_type`` has no asset identity (orchestration
63	    providers) or a required key is missing/empty on either dict — never
64	    returns an empty namespace or name.
65	    """
66	    if conn_type == "snowflake":
67	        return _resolve_snowflake(config, target)
68	    if conn_type == "unity_catalog":
69	        return _resolve_unity_catalog(config, target)
70	    if conn_type == "adls_gen2":
71	        return _resolve_adls_gen2(config, target)
72	    if conn_type == "s3":
73	        return _resolve_s3(config, target)
74	    if conn_type == "iceberg":
75	        return _resolve_iceberg(config, target)
76	    raise ValueError(f"connection type {conn_type!r} has no asset identity (not a datasource)")
77	
78	
79	def normalize_snowflake_account(account: str) -> str:
80	    """Normalize a Snowflake account identifier (openlineage's ``fix_account_name``).
81	
82	    Byte-compatible with openlineage-common (ADR 0034 decision 2). The hyphen
83	    check is scoped to the **first dot-segment** only: an org-account form
84	    (``{org}-{account}``) returns *just that first segment*, dropping anything
85	    after a dot. Otherwise the locator is dot-segmented and defaulted — one
86	    segment (bare locator) gets ``.us-west-1.aws`` appended; two segments
87	    (locator + region — the region legitimately contains hyphens like
88	    ``us-east-1``) get ``.aws`` appended; three or more segments are already
89	    complete and pass through unchanged.
90	    """
91	    account = account.strip()
92	    if not account:
93	        raise ValueError("snowflake account must be non-empty")
94	    parts = account.split(".")
95	    if "-" in parts[0]:
96	        return parts[0]
97	    if len(parts) == 1:
98	        return f"{parts[0]}.us-west-1.aws"
99	    if len(parts) == 2:
100	        return f"{parts[0]}.{parts[1]}.aws"
101	    return account
102	
103	
104	def format_snowflake_name(database: str, schema: str, table: str) -> str:
105	    """Join a Snowflake ``DB.SCHEMA.TABLE`` into the OpenLineage ``name`` string.
106	
107	    Each part is folded to the engine's unquoted case (upper) unless double-quote
108	    wrapped, exactly as :func:`_normalize_part` does — the one place that rule
109	    lives, reused by both the suite-target resolver here and the dbt-manifest
110	    canonicalizer (`lineage.edges`) so their identifiers match byte-for-byte.
111	    """
112	    return ".".join(_normalize_part(part, engine="snowflake") for part in (database, schema, table))
113	
114	
115	def format_unity_catalog_name(catalog: str, schema: str, table: str) -> str:
116	    """Join a Unity Catalog ``catalog.schema.table`` into the OpenLineage ``name``.
117	
118	    Each part folds to the engine's unquoted case (lower) unless quote-wrapped,
119	    exactly as :func:`_normalize_part` does — the one place that rule lives, reused
120	    by both the suite-target resolver here and the dbt-manifest canonicalizer
121	    (`lineage.edges`) for databricks/spark adapters so identifiers match byte-for-byte.
122	    """
123	    return ".".join(
124	        _normalize_part(part, engine="unity_catalog") for part in (catalog, schema, table)
125	    )
126	
127	
128	def _resolve_snowflake(config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]) -> AssetIdentity:
129	    account = _require(config, "account", "snowflake", "config")
130	    database = _require(config, "database", "snowflake", "config")
131	    schema = _str_or_none(target.get("schema")) or _str_or_none(config.get("schema"))
132	    if not schema:
133	        raise ValueError("snowflake asset identity requires a 'schema' (target or config)")
134	    table = _require(target, "table", "snowflake", "target")
135	    namespace = f"snowflake://{normalize_snowflake_account(account)}"
136	    name = format_snowflake_name(database, schema, table)
137	    return AssetIdentity(namespace=namespace, name=name)
138	
139	
140	def _resolve_unity_catalog(config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]) -> AssetIdentity:
141	    workspace_url = _require(config, "workspace_url", "unity_catalog", "config")
142	    netloc = _url_host(workspace_url)
143	    if not netloc:
144	        raise ValueError("unity_catalog asset identity requires a valid 'workspace_url'")
145	    catalog = _require(target, "catalog", "unity_catalog", "target")
146	    schema = _str_or_none(target.get("schema")) or "default"
147	    table = _require(target, "table", "unity_catalog", "target")
148	    namespace = f"unitycatalog://{netloc}"
149	    name = format_unity_catalog_name(catalog, schema, table)
150	    return AssetIdentity(namespace=namespace, name=name)
151	
152	
153	def _resolve_adls_gen2(config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]) -> AssetIdentity:
154	    container = _require(config, "container", "adls_gen2", "config")
155	    account_url = _require(config, "account_url", "adls_gen2", "config")
156	    host = _url_host(account_url)
157	    account = host.split(".")[0] if host else ""
158	    if not account:
159	        raise ValueError("adls_gen2 asset identity requires a valid 'account_url'")
160	    namespace = f"abfss://{container}@{account}.dfs.core.windows.net"
161	    name = _flatfile_name(target, "adls_gen2")
162	    return AssetIdentity(namespace=namespace, name=name)
163	
164	
165	def _resolve_s3(config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]) -> AssetIdentity:
166	    bucket = _require(config, "bucket", "s3", "config")
167	    namespace = f"s3://{bucket}"
168	    name = _flatfile_name(target, "s3")
169	    return AssetIdentity(namespace=namespace, name=name)
170	
171	
172	def _resolve_iceberg(config: dict[str, Any], target: dict[str, Any]) -> AssetIdentity:
173	    catalog_uri = config.get("catalog_uri")
174	    namespace = (
175	        catalog_uri.strip() if isinstance(catalog_uri, str) and catalog_uri.strip() else "file"
176	    )
177	    table = _require(target, "table", "iceberg", "target")
178	    ns_part = _str_or_none(target.get("namespace"))
179	    name = f"{ns_part}.{table}" if ns_part else table
180	    return AssetIdentity(namespace=namespace, name=name)
181	
182	
183	def _flatfile_name(target: dict[str, Any], conn_type: str) -> str:
184	    path = _str_or_none(target.get("path"))
185	    if path:
186	        return path.lstrip("/")
187	    pattern = _str_or_none(target.get("pattern"))
188	    if pattern:
189	        return _pattern_base_prefix(pattern)
190	    raise ValueError(f"{conn_type} asset identity requires a target 'path' or 'pattern'")
191	
192	
193	def _pattern_base_prefix(pattern: str) -> str:
194	    """The literal directory prefix in front of the first regex metacharacter.
195	
196	    ``pattern`` is a **regex** (flatfile.py `re.compile`s it, first capture group
197	    = batch key), so cut at the first regex metacharacter and keep the literal
198	    text before it (the Spark flat-file-dataset convention: the asset is the
199	    directory, not the per-file match). If that literal prefix contains a ``/``,
200	    truncate to just after the last one (the directory); if it has no ``/`` but
201	    is non-empty, use it as-is; if it is empty (a metacharacter leads the
202	    pattern), fall back to the whole pattern verbatim rather than an empty name.
203	    """
204	    match = _REGEX_METACHARS.search(pattern)
205	    prefix = pattern[: match.start()] if match else pattern
206	    if "/" in prefix:
207	        base = prefix[: prefix.rfind("/") + 1]
208	    elif prefix:
209	        base = prefix
210	    else:
211	        base = pattern
212	    return base.lstrip("/")
213	
214	
215	def _normalize_part(part: str, *, engine: str) -> str:
216	    """Fold one dotted-name segment to the engine's unquoted-identifier case.
217	
218	    A double-quote-wrapped (Snowflake) or double-quote/backtick-wrapped (UC)
219	    part keeps its inner case verbatim once the quotes are stripped — the
220	    engine-returned case for a quoted identifier. An unquoted part is folded
221	    to the case the engine's catalog would report it in (Snowflake upper, UC
222	    lower).
223	    """
224	    quote_chars = ('"',) if engine == "snowflake" else ('"', "`")
225	    for quote in quote_chars:
226	        if len(part) >= 2 and part.startswith(quote) and part.endswith(quote):
227	            inner = part[1:-1]
228	            if not inner:
229	                # A quoted-empty identifier (`""`) slips past _require (the raw
230	                # value is non-empty) but yields an empty dotted segment — reject
231	                # it rather than key an asset on a malformed name like `DB.SCHEMA.`.
232	                raise ValueError("identifier part is empty after stripping quotes")
233	            return inner
234	    return part.upper() if engine == "snowflake" else part.lower()
235	
236	
237	def _url_host(url: str) -> str:
238	    """The host of ``url``, tolerating a scheme-less value.
239	
240	    ``urlparse`` puts a scheme-less host (``adb-1234.azuredatabricks.net``) in
241	    ``path``, not ``netloc``; fall back to the first ``/``-segment of ``path`` so
242	    a valid-but-scheme-less workspace/account URL still resolves a host.
243	    """
244	    parsed = urlparse(url)
245	    return parsed.netloc or parsed.path.split("/", 1)[0]
246	
247	
248	def _require(d: dict[str, Any], field: str, conn_type: str, kind: str) -> str:
249	    """Require a non-empty string ``field`` on config/target dict ``d`` (``kind``)."""
250	    value = d.get(field)
251	    if not isinstance(value, str) or not value.strip():
252	        raise ValueError(f"{conn_type} asset identity requires a non-empty {kind} {field!r}")
253	    return value
254	
255	
256	def _str_or_none(value: Any) -> str | None:
257	    return value if isinstance(value, str) and value.strip() else None
258

1	"""Read-only asset view — the browse/reason surface over `assets` (ADR 0034, #760).
2	
3	Assets are what users *reason about*; suites remain how checks *execute* (ADR 0034
4	guiding principle). This module aggregates, per asset, the suites that target it +
5	their latest run health + the lineage neighbourhood, for the `/assets` API.
6	
7	**Authz is derived, never granted (ADR 0034 decision 5 / ADR 0027).** An asset is
8	visible iff the caller can `view` ≥1 suite mapped to it (`suites.asset_id`); the
9	aggregation is filtered to *only* the suites the caller's grants cover; a
10	workspace-admin sees every suite (`include_all`). An asset wholly outside the
11	caller's grants is 404-no-leak (the API layer raises `AssetNotFoundError`). This
12	reuses `suite_service.accessible_suite_ids` verbatim, so the visibility rule has a
13	single source of truth and can never drift from the suites/runs surfaces.
14	
15	Asset-metadata mutation (owner, description) is workspace-Admin-only — enforced at
16	the API layer (`require_workspace_admin`), not here; `update_asset_metadata` is the
17	plain persistence half.
18	
19	Lineage nodes are **not** authz-filtered: blast radius is the point (ADR 0034 §2),
20	and a node exposes only its OpenLineage `(namespace, name)` + whether it is
21	monitored — never run data or grants — so nothing sensitive leaks through it.
22	"""

48	
49	    status_code = 404
50	    code = "asset_not_found"
51	
52	
53	class AssetOwnerInvalidError(DataQError):
54	    """The `owner_user_id` on a metadata update names no existing user — checked
55	    up front (the share-grant FK-precheck idiom, `share_service.grant_share`) so a
56	    bad id is a clean 422, never a raw FK IntegrityError surfacing as 500."""
57	
58	    status_code = 422
59	    code = "asset_owner_invalid"
60	
61	
62	@dataclass(frozen=True)
63	class RunOutcome:
64	    """A suite's latest run outcome — execution status + the data-quality summary.
65	
66	    ``worst_severity`` is the highest failing tier among evaluated checks (warn <
67	    fail < critical), or ``None`` when all passed / nothing evaluated. ``run_id`` /
68	    timestamps are ``None`` when the suite has never run."""
69	
70	    run_id: uuid.UUID | None = None
71	    status: str | None = None
72	    worst_severity: str | None = None
73	    checks_total: int = 0
74	    checks_passed: int = 0
75	    finished_at: datetime | None = None
76	    created_at: datetime | None = None
77	
78	
79	@dataclass(frozen=True)
80	class ComposingSuite:
81	    """One suite the caller can see that targets the asset, with its latest run."""
82	
83	    suite_id: uuid.UUID
84	    name: str
85	    my_permission: str
86	    latest_run: RunOutcome
87	
88	
89	@dataclass(frozen=True)
90	class AssetSummary:
91	    """List-row aggregation for one visible asset."""
92	
93	    id: uuid.UUID
94	    namespace: str
95	    name: str
96	    env: str | None
97	    description: str | None
98	    owner_user_id: uuid.UUID | None
99	    last_seen: datetime
100	    suite_count: int
101	    # Health rolled up across the caller-visible composing suites' latest runs.
102	    worst_severity: str | None
103	    checks_total: int
104	    checks_passed: int
105	    last_run_at: datetime | None
106	    # Latest-run *execution* states (distinct from check severity — a run can fail
107	    # operationally and write no results, so severity alone would read green):
108	    # any latest run `failed` / any latest run still `queued`/`running`. The UI
109	    # health badge consumes these so an operational failure is never shown passing.
110	    has_failed_run: bool = False
111	    has_active_run: bool = False
112	
113	
114	@dataclass(frozen=True)
115	class LineageNode:
116	    """A lineage neighbour — enough to render, no run data (ADR 0034 §2)."""
117	
118	    id: uuid.UUID
119	    namespace: str
120	    name: str
121	    env: str | None
122	    is_monitored: bool
123	
124	
125	@dataclass(frozen=True)
126	class AssetDetail:
127	    """Asset detail: the summary aggregation + per-suite breakdown + lineage."""
128	
129	    summary: AssetSummary
130	    suites: list[ComposingSuite]
131	    upstream: list[LineageNode] = field(default_factory=list)
132	    downstream: list[LineageNode] = field(default_factory=list)

135	# ── internals ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
136	
137	
138	def _latest_run_per_suite(session: Session, suite_ids: list[uuid.UUID]) -> dict[uuid.UUID, Run]:
139	    """The most-recent run for each suite (DISTINCT ON, newest `created_at`)."""
140	    if not suite_ids:
141	        return {}
142	    rows = session.scalars(
143	        select(Run)
144	        .where(Run.suite_id.in_(suite_ids))
145	        .distinct(Run.suite_id)
146	        .order_by(Run.suite_id, Run.created_at.desc())
147	    )
148	    return {run.suite_id: run for run in rows}
149	
150	
151	def _run_outcome(run: Run | None, outcome: tuple[int, int, str | None] | None) -> RunOutcome:
152	    """Assemble a `RunOutcome` from a suite's latest run + its check-outcome tuple."""
153	    if run is None:
154	        return RunOutcome()
155	    total, passed, worst = outcome or (0, 0, None)
156	    return RunOutcome(
157	        run_id=run.id,
158	        status=run.status,
159	        worst_severity=worst,
160	        checks_total=total,
161	        checks_passed=passed,
162	        finished_at=run.finished_at,
163	        created_at=run.created_at,
164	    )
165	
166	
167	def _composing_suites(
168	    suites: list[Suite],
169	    levels: dict[uuid.UUID, str | None],
170	    latest_runs: dict[uuid.UUID, Run],
171	    outcomes: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[int, int, str | None]],
172	) -> list[ComposingSuite]:
173	    """Build the per-suite breakdown for one asset's suites (sorted by name)."""
174	    composing: list[ComposingSuite] = []
175	    for suite in suites:
176	        level = levels.get(suite.id)
177	        if level is None:  # defensive: only reachable suites are passed in
178	            continue
179	        run = latest_runs.get(suite.id)
180	        outcome = outcomes.get(run.id) if run is not None else None
181	        composing.append(
182	            ComposingSuite(
183	                suite_id=suite.id,
184	                name=suite.name,
185	                my_permission=level,
186	                latest_run=_run_outcome(run, outcome),
187	            )
188	        )
189	    return composing
190	
191	
192	def _roll_up(asset: Asset, composing: list[ComposingSuite]) -> AssetSummary:
193	    """Roll a set of composing suites up into the asset-level health summary."""
194	    statuses: list[str] = []
195	    checks_total = checks_passed = 0
196	    last_run_at: datetime | None = None
197	    has_failed_run = has_active_run = False
198	    for suite in composing:
199	        run = suite.latest_run
200	        if run.worst_severity is not None:
201	            statuses.append(run.worst_severity)
202	        # Execution state, distinct from check severity (see AssetSummary): a
203	        # `failed` run wrote no results and must not roll up green; an active run
204	        # hasn't concluded yet.
205	        if run.status == "failed":
206	            has_failed_run = True
207	        elif run.status in ("queued", "running"):
208	            has_active_run = True
209	        checks_total += run.checks_total
210	        checks_passed += run.checks_passed
211	        ts = run.finished_at or run.created_at
212	        if ts is not None and (last_run_at is None or ts > last_run_at):
213	            last_run_at = ts
214	    return AssetSummary(
215	        id=asset.id,
216	        namespace=asset.namespace,
217	        name=asset.name,
218	        env=asset.env,
219	        description=asset.description,
220	        owner_user_id=asset.owner_user_id,
221	        last_seen=asset.last_seen,
222	        suite_count=len(composing),
223	        worst_severity=worst_severity(statuses),
224	        checks_total=checks_total,
225	        checks_passed=checks_passed,
226	        last_run_at=last_run_at,
227	        has_failed_run=has_failed_run,
228	        has_active_run=has_active_run,
229	    )
230	
231	
232	# ── public API ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
233	
234	
235	def list_visible_assets(
236	    session: Session,
237	    *,
238	    user_id: uuid.UUID,
239	    include_all: bool = False,
240	    limit: int = 200,
241	    offset: int = 0,
242	) -> list[AssetSummary]:
243	    """Assets the caller can see (via ≥1 accessible composing suite), sorted by
244	    ``(namespace, name)`` and paginated with ``limit``/``offset``.
245	
246	    Pagination is applied at the SQL level over the *asset* page (not the suite
247	    rows), so a page is a stable, deterministic slice regardless of how many
248	    suites compose each asset. Aggregation is filtered to the caller's grants: a
249	    partial-grant caller sees the asset but only the suites/runs they can view
250	    roll up into its health."""
251	    accessible = accessible_suite_ids(user_id, include_all=include_all)
252	    visible_asset_ids = select(Suite.asset_id).where(
253	        Suite.asset_id.is_not(None), Suite.id.in_(accessible)
254	    )
255	    assets = list(
256	        session.scalars(
257	            select(Asset)
258	            .where(Asset.id.in_(visible_asset_ids))
259	            .order_by(Asset.namespace, Asset.name)
260	            .limit(limit)
261	            .offset(offset)
262	        )
263	    )
264	    if not assets:
265	        return []
266	
267	    suites = list(
268	        session.scalars(
269	            select(Suite)
270	            .where(Suite.asset_id.in_([a.id for a in assets]), Suite.id.in_(accessible))
271	            .order_by(Suite.name)
272	        )
273	    )
274	    levels = effective_permissions(session, suites, user_id)
275	    latest_runs = _latest_run_per_suite(session, [s.id for s in suites])
276	    outcomes = check_outcome_counts(session, [r.id for r in latest_runs.values()])
277	
278	    suites_by_asset: dict[uuid.UUID, list[Suite]] = defaultdict(list)
279	    for suite in suites:
280	        assert suite.asset_id is not None  # filtered in the query
281	        suites_by_asset[suite.asset_id].append(suite)
282	
283	    return [
284	        _roll_up(
285	            asset,
286	            _composing_suites(suites_by_asset.get(asset.id, []), levels, latest_runs, outcomes),
287	        )
288	        for asset in assets
289	    ]
290	
291	
292	def get_visible_asset(
293	    session: Session, asset_id: uuid.UUID, *, user_id: uuid.UUID, include_all: bool = False
294	) -> AssetDetail:
295	    """One asset's detail (aggregation + per-suite breakdown + lineage).
296	
297	    Raises `AssetNotFoundError` (404) if the asset does not exist or the caller can
298	    view no suite targeting it — the two are indistinguishable (no-leak). A
299	    workspace-admin (``include_all``) sees every asset, **including suite-less
300	    orphans** (e.g. a lineage-only node, or an asset whose last composing suite was
301	    deleted) — for them only a truly unknown id 404s."""
302	    asset = session.get(Asset, asset_id)
303	    accessible = accessible_suite_ids(user_id, include_all=include_all)
304	    suites = list(
305	        session.scalars(
306	            select(Suite)
307	            .where(Suite.asset_id == asset_id, Suite.id.in_(accessible))
308	            .order_by(Suite.name)
309	        )
310	    )
311	    # No-leak: for a non-admin, an unknown id and an id they can see no suite for
312	    # both 404. An admin's visibility is workspace-wide (ADR 0027), so a suite-less
313	    # asset is still returned (with an empty suites list) rather than hidden.
314	    if asset is None or (not suites and not include_all):
315	        raise AssetNotFoundError("asset not found", detail={"asset_id": str(asset_id)})
316	
317	    levels = effective_permissions(session, suites, user_id)
318	    latest_runs = _latest_run_per_suite(session, [s.id for s in suites])
319	    outcomes = check_outcome_counts(session, [r.id for r in latest_runs.values()])
320	    composing = _composing_suites(suites, levels, latest_runs, outcomes)
321	
322	    summary = _roll_up(asset, composing)
323	    upstream = _lineage_nodes(session, upstream_assets(session, asset_id))
324	    downstream = _lineage_nodes(session, downstream_assets(session, asset_id))
325	    return AssetDetail(summary=summary, suites=composing, upstream=upstream, downstream=downstream)
326	
327	
328	def summarize_asset(
329	    session: Session, asset: Asset, *, user_id: uuid.UUID, include_all: bool = False
330	) -> AssetSummary:
331	    """Roll one already-loaded asset up into its list-row summary.
332	
333	    Unlike `get_visible_asset`, this never 404s on "no composing suites" — an asset
334	    with zero suites rolls up to an empty (no-run) health summary. Used by the
335	    admin PATCH response, where the asset need not have suites to have metadata."""
336	    accessible = accessible_suite_ids(user_id, include_all=include_all)
337	    suites = list(
338	        session.scalars(
339	            select(Suite)
340	            .where(Suite.asset_id == asset.id, Suite.id.in_(accessible))
341	            .order_by(Suite.name)
342	        )
343	    )
344	    levels = effective_permissions(session, suites, user_id) if suites else {}
345	    latest_runs = _latest_run_per_suite(session, [s.id for s in suites])
346	    outcomes = check_outcome_counts(session, [r.id for r in latest_runs.values()])
347	    composing = _composing_suites(suites, levels, latest_runs, outcomes)
348	    return _roll_up(asset, composing)
349	
350	
351	def _lineage_nodes(session: Session, assets: list[Asset]) -> list[LineageNode]:
352	    """Map reachable lineage assets to render-only nodes (+ monitored flag).
353	
354	    ``is_monitored`` = the asset has ≥1 suite targeting it (globally — a structural
355	    fact, not a grant), resolved for all nodes in one grouped query (no N+1)."""
356	    if not assets:
357	        return []
358	    ids = [a.id for a in assets]
359	    monitored = {
360	        asset_id
361	        for (asset_id,) in session.execute(
362	            select(Suite.asset_id).where(Suite.asset_id.in_(ids)).group_by(Suite.asset_id)
363	        )
364	    }
365	    return [
366	        LineageNode(
367	            id=a.id,
368	            namespace=a.namespace,
369	            name=a.name,
370	            env=a.env,
371	            is_monitored=a.id in monitored,
372	        )
373	        for a in assets
374	    ]
375	
376	
377	def update_asset_metadata(
378	    session: Session,
379	    asset_id: uuid.UUID,
380	    *,
381	    owner_user_id: uuid.UUID | None = None,
382	    description: str | None = None,
383	    set_owner: bool = False,
384	    set_description: bool = False,
385	) -> Asset:
386	    """Set an asset's owner and/or description (workspace-Admin-only; gated at API).
387	
388	    ``set_owner`` / ``set_description`` are the partial-update discriminators, so a
389	    field can be explicitly cleared to ``None`` versus left untouched (mirrors the
390	    suite PATCH's None-means-leave-alone problem, made explicit). Raises
391	    `AssetNotFoundError` (404) for an unknown id — no authz derivation here, since a
392	    workspace-admin sees every asset (ADR 0027) — and `AssetOwnerInvalidError` (422)
393	    when ``owner_user_id`` names no existing user (FK pre-check, never a raw 500)."""
394	    asset = session.get(Asset, asset_id)
395	    if asset is None:
396	        raise AssetNotFoundError("asset not found", detail={"asset_id": str(asset_id)})
397	    if set_owner:
398	        if owner_user_id is not None and session.get(User, owner_user_id) is None:
399	            raise AssetOwnerInvalidError(
400	                "owner user does not exist", detail={"owner_user_id": str(owner_user_id)}
401	            )
402	        asset.owner_user_id = owner_user_id
403	    if set_description:
404	        asset.description = description
405	    session.commit()
406	    session.refresh(asset)
407	    log.info("asset_metadata_updated", asset_id=str(asset.id))
408	    return asset

341:def check_outcome_counts(
def check_outcome_counts(
    session: Session, run_ids: Sequence[uuid.UUID]
) -> dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[int, int, str | None]]:
    """Per-run ``(checks_total, checks_passed, worst_severity)`` for a set of runs,
    in a single grouped query (no N+1). ``worst_severity`` is the highest of
    warn/fail/critical present, else ``None`` (all passed / only operational).

    ``checks_total``/``checks_passed`` count **evaluated** checks — the four
    severity tiers (pass/warn/fail/critical) — and **exclude** operational
    ``skip``/``error`` (#122), so the X/Y matches the run-detail page's "Checks
    passed" denominator and an all-skip run reports total 0 (rendered ``—``, not a
    misleading green ``0/N``).

    Lets the runs list surface a run's *data-quality* outcome — distinct from the
    run's *execution* status, which is ``succeeded`` even when checks failed."""
    if not run_ids:
        return {}
    rows = session.execute(
        select(Result.run_id, Result.status, func.count())
        .where(Result.run_id.in_(run_ids))
        .group_by(Result.run_id, Result.status)
    ).all()
    by_run: dict[uuid.UUID, dict[str, int]] = defaultdict(dict)
    for run_id, status, n in rows:
        by_run[run_id][status] = n
    out: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[int, int, str | None]] = {}
    for run_id, by_status in by_run.items():
        passed = by_status.get("pass", 0)
        # Worst check outcome via the single shared severity helper (#655); skip/error
        # aren't failing tiers, so they never rank.
        worst = worst_severity(by_status)
        # Evaluated checks only: pass + the three failing tiers (skip/error excluded).
        total = passed + sum(by_status.get(tier, 0) for tier in SEVERITY_RANK)
        out[run_id] = (total, passed, worst)
    return out


def get_run(session: Session, run_id: uuid.UUID) -> Run | None:
    """Fetch a run by id (no authz — the API layer gates on the run's suite)."""
    return session.get(Run, run_id)


_TERMINAL_STATUSES = frozenset({"succeeded", "failed", "cancelled"})
# The complement, single-sourced from the canonical status set so a new
# lifecycle status can't silently escape the reaper's net (#309).
_NON_TERMINAL_STATUSES = frozenset(RUN_STATUSES) - _TERMINAL_STATUSES

content.text: Now I understand the vocab: run `status` (queued/running/succeeded/failed/cancelled) vs result `status` (severity tiers + operational `skip`/`error`). A run can be `succeeded` while individual checks `error` — so `has_failed_run` alone misses per-check operational errors. To honor "error/skip results feed connection health," I need error/skip presence on the summary. Let me map the full consumer surface.

        "run_completed",
        run_id=str(run.id),
        suite_success=all(o.success for o in outcomes),
        n_results=len(rows),
    )
    return run


def skip_run(session: Session, *, run: Run, checks: list[Check], reason: str) -> Run:
    """Record a run that had nothing to evaluate — every check `skip`ped (#122).

    Used when the adapter is never invoked because there's no data to validate
    (e.g. the target batch hasn't landed yet). The run still **succeeds** — it
    executed end to end, it just found nothing to check — and each check gets a
    ``skip`` Result carrying the ``reason`` (operational, not a severity tier, so
    it's excluded from the health-score N per ADR 0005). Distinct from ``failed``,
    which means the run could not execute.
    """
    run.status = "running"
    run.started_at = _now()
    session.commit()
    rows = [
        Result(run_id=run.id, check_id=check.id, status="skip", observed_value={"reason": reason})
        for check in checks
    ]
    session.add_all(rows)
    run.status = "succeeded"
    run.finished_at = _now()
    session.commit()

input.new_string:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AssetSummary:
    """List-row aggregation for one visible asset.

    **Two orthogonal health axes (#803).** The old single "health" conflated them:

    - *Suite health* (data quality, ADR 0005) — ``worst_severity`` over the
      **evaluated** checks, plus ``checks_total``/``checks_passed``. Operational
      results never rank here, so a datasource DataQ couldn't reach reads as "no
      data", never as a green "passing" nor as a red data failure.
    - *Connection health* (reachability) — ``has_operational_error`` /
      ``has_skip``: could DataQ execute against the datasource at all? Derived
      **purely from the runs already recorded** (a latest run that `failed`, or
      any ``error``/``skip`` result on one) — there is no connection-probe polling
      loop behind this, by design.
    """

    id: uuid.UUID
    namespace: str
    name: str
    env: str | None
    description: str | None
    owner_user_id: uuid.UUID | None
    last_seen: datetime
    suite_count: int
    # ── suite health (data quality) ──
    worst_severity: str | None
    checks_total: int
    checks_passed: int
    last_run_at: datetime | None
    # ── connection health (reachability / execution) ──
    # `has_failed_run`: any latest run whose *execution* `failed` (wrote no results).
    # `has_active_run`: any latest run still `queued`/`running` (hasn't concluded).
    # `has_operational_error`: a failed run OR any `error` result — DataQ could not
    #   evaluate against the datasource. `has_skip`: any `skip` result (a
    #   precondition, e.g. the batch hasn't landed, wasn't met) — degraded, not down.
    has_failed_run: bool = False
    has_active_run: bool = False
    has_operational_error: bool = False
    has_skip: bool = False

    """Assemble a `RunOutcome` from a suite's latest run + its check-outcome tuple."""
    if run is None:
        return RunOutcome()
    total, passed, worst = outcome or (0, 0, None)
    return RunOutcome(
        run_id=run.id,
        status=run.status,
        worst_severity=worst,
        checks_total=total,
        checks_passed=passed,

    )


def _composing_suites(
    suites: list[Suite],
    levels: dict[uuid.UUID, str | None],
    latest_runs: dict[uuid.UUID, Run],
    outcomes: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[int, int, str | None]],
) -> list[ComposingSuite]:
    """Build the per-suite breakdown for one asset's suites (sorted by name)."""
    composing: list[ComposingSuite] = []
    for suite in suites:
        level = levels.get(suite.id)
        if level is None:  # defensive: only reachable suites are passed in
            continue
        run = latest_runs.get(suite.id)
        outcome = outcomes.get(run.id) if run is not None else None
        composing.append(
            ComposingSuite(
                suite_id=suite.id,
                name=suite.name,
                my_permission=level,
                latest_run=_run_outcome(run, outcome),
            )
        )
    return composing

def _run_outcome(
    run: Run | None,
    outcome: tuple[int, int, str | None] | None,
    op_flags: tuple[bool, bool] | None = None,
) -> RunOutcome:
    """Assemble a `RunOutcome` from a suite's latest run + its check-outcome tuple
    + its operational (`error`/`skip`) flags."""
    if run is None:
        return RunOutcome()
    total, passed, worst = outcome or (0, 0, None)
    has_error, has_skip = op_flags or (False, False)
    return RunOutcome(
        run_id=run.id,
        status=run.status,
        worst_severity=worst,
        checks_total=total,
        checks_passed=passed,
        finished_at=run.finished_at,
        created_at=run.created_at,
        has_error=has_error,
        has_skip=has_skip,
    )


def _composing_suites(
    suites: list[Suite],
    levels: dict[uuid.UUID, str | None],
    latest_runs: dict[uuid.UUID, Run],
    outcomes: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[int, int, str | None]],
    op_flags: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[bool, bool]] | None = None,
) -> list[ComposingSuite]:
    """Build the per-suite breakdown for one asset's suites (sorted by name)."""
    op_flags = op_flags or {}
    composing: list[ComposingSuite] = []
    for suite in suites:
        level = levels.get(suite.id)
        if level is None:  # defensive: only reachable suites are passed in
            continue
        run = latest_runs.get(suite.id)
        outcome = outcomes.get(run.id) if run is not None else None
        flags = op_flags.get(run.id) if run is not None else None
        composing.append(
            ComposingSuite(
                suite_id=suite.id,
                name=suite.name,
                my_permission=level,
                latest_run=_run_outcome(run, outcome, flags),
            )
        )
    return composing

input.new_string:
class AssetSummaryRead(ApiModel):
    """List-row aggregation for one visible asset, carrying **two orthogonal health
    axes** (#803) the UI renders separately:

    - *Suite health* (data quality) — `worst_severity` / `checks_*` over the
      **evaluated** checks of the caller-visible composing suites' latest runs;
      `worst_severity` is null when all passed or nothing has run. Operational
      results never rank here.
    - *Connection health* (reachability) — `has_operational_error` / `has_skip`
      (plus the execution states below): could DataQ execute against the
      datasource at all? Derived from the recorded runs only — no connection-probe
      polling loop.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)

    id: uuid.UUID
    namespace: str
    name: str
    env: str | None
    description: str | None
    owner_user_id: uuid.UUID | None
    last_seen: datetime
    suite_count: int
    worst_severity: str | None
    checks_total: int
    checks_passed: int
    last_run_at: datetime | None
    # Latest-run execution states (distinct from check severity): any composing
    # suite's latest run `failed` / still `queued`/`running`.
    has_failed_run: bool
    has_active_run: bool
    # Connection health (#803): a failed run OR any `error` result → DataQ could not
    # evaluate against the datasource; `skip` → a precondition wasn't met (degraded).
    has_operational_error: bool
    has_skip: bool

# ── connection health vs suite health (#803) ─────────────────────────────────
#
# The two axes must not bleed into each other: operational `error`/`skip` results
# (#122) feed *connection* health (could DataQ reach the datasource?) and are
# invisible to *suite* health (is the data good?), which is severity-only.


def _suite_with_run(db: Any, owner: User, *, run_status: str, result_statuses: list[str]) -> Asset:
    """A suite on a fresh asset with one run carrying `result_statuses` results."""
    conn = _conn(db, owner)
    suite = suite_service.create_suite(
        db,
        name=f"S-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
        description=None,
        connection_id=conn.id,
        created_by=owner.id,
        target={"table": f"T{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}"},
    )
    run = Run(suite_id=suite.id, status=run_status, triggered_by="manual")
    db.add(run)
    db.flush()
    for status in result_statuses:
        check = Check(
            suite_id=suite.id,
            name=f"c-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
            expectation_type="expect_column_to_exist",
            kwargs={"column": "X"},
        )
        db.add(check)
        db.flush()
        db.add(Result(run_id=run.id, check_id=check.id, status=status))
    db.commit()
    assert suite.asset_id is not None
    return db.get(Asset, suite.asset_id)


def test_error_result_feeds_connection_health_not_suite_health(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A run that SUCCEEDED but whose check threw: connection health is degraded
    (operational error) while suite health stays severity-free — the exact case
    `has_failed_run` alone misses, since the run itself never failed."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["error"])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_operational_error is True  # connection axis: could not evaluate
    assert s.has_failed_run is False  # the run itself succeeded
    assert s.worst_severity is None  # suite axis: no DQ verdict at all
    assert s.checks_total == 0  # `error` is not an evaluated check


def test_skip_result_is_degraded_not_an_error(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """`skip` = a precondition wasn't met (the batch hasn't landed). The run
    executed, so it is NOT an operational error — only a degraded connection."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["skip"])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_skip is True
    assert s.has_operational_error is False
    assert s.worst_severity is None
    assert s.checks_total == 0


def test_failed_run_is_an_operational_error(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A run whose execution failed wrote no results at all — connection axis."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(db_session, owner, run_status="failed", result_statuses=[])
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.has_failed_run is True
    assert s.has_operational_error is True
    assert s.worst_severity is None


def test_failing_data_does_not_touch_connection_health(db_session: Any) -> None:
    """The mirror case: the datasource was perfectly reachable, the DATA is bad.
    Suite health goes red; connection health stays clean."""
    owner = _user(db_session)
    asset = _suite_with_run(
        db_session, owner, run_status="succeeded", result_statuses=["pass", "critical"]
    )
    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

    assert s.worst_severity == "critical"  # suite axis: data is bad
    assert s.checks_total == 2 and s.checks_passed == 1
    assert s.has_operational_error is False  # connection axis: nothing wrong here
    assert s.has_skip is False


def test_operational_result_flags_empty_input() -> None:
    assert run_service.operational_result_flags(None, []) == {}  # type: ignore[arg-type]

input.old_string:
- **Asset browse** — two lenses over the same assets: a **By source** drill-down
  (datasource → database/catalog → schema → table, with env + health on each leaf) and a
  flat, searchable **All assets** table. Both open the asset detail (health across suites,
  lineage, incidents).
input.new_string:
- **Asset browse** — two lenses over the same assets: a **By source** drill-down
  (datasource → database/catalog → schema → table, with env + health on each leaf) and a
  flat, searchable **All assets** table. Both open the asset detail (health across suites,
  lineage, incidents).
- **Two health axes on an asset** — *Connection health* ("could DataQ reach and execute
  against the datasource?" — a failed run, or a check the datasource threw on) and
  *Data-quality health* (the severity-weighted verdict of its suites). They are derived
  and shown separately, so a datasource DataQ cannot connect to never masquerades as a
  data failure — and a run that evaluated nothing reads **No data**, never a green
  "passing". Operational `error`/`skip` results feed only the connection axis. Both are
  read off the runs already recorded — there is no connection-probe polling loop.

input.command:
cd /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ
git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(assets): split asset health into connection vs data-quality axes (#803)

The single asset "Health" conflated two different questions: could DataQ reach
the datasource at all, and is the data any good. A datasource DataQ could not
connect to therefore rendered as a data failure.

Split them into two derived, separately-labelled axes on AssetDetail:

- Connection health — could DataQ execute against the datasource? A latest run
  whose execution `failed`, or any check the datasource threw on (`error`), reads
  Errors; a `skip` (precondition unmet, e.g. the batch hasn't landed) reads
  Degraded; a clean concluded run reads Reachable.
- Data-quality health — the ADR 0005 severity-weighted verdict of the suites.
  Operational `error`/`skip` results never rank here (#122 semantics).

Backend: `operational_result_flags` (the exact complement of
`check_outcome_counts`, which drops skip/error) rolls up to two new
AssetSummary fields, `has_operational_error` + `has_skip`. Derived entirely from
runs already recorded — no connection-probe polling loop, by design.

Suite health now keys "Passing" off `checks_total > 0` (evaluated checks) rather
than "a run happened", so a run that evaluated nothing reports "No data" instead
of a green Passing it hasn't earned. The dashboard needs-attention rule moves to
`has_operational_error`, which additionally catches a run that *succeeded* while
its checks errored — a case the old run-status-only rule read as healthy.

Fixes #803

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
git push -u origin feature/issue-803-asset-health-split -q 2>&1 | tail -1
gh pr create --title "feat(assets): split asset health into connection vs data-quality axes (#803)" --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What & why

Fixes #803. The asset **Health** signal conflated two different questions — *could DataQ reach the datasource?* and *is the data good?* — so a datasource DataQ couldn't connect to rendered as a data failure.

Live proof of the split (the seeded ORDERS asset is **both** unreachable *and* has bad data — previously you'd only ever have seen "Critical"):

> **Connection** `Errors`  ·  **Data quality** `Critical`

## The two axes

- **Connection health** — could DataQ *execute* against the datasource? A latest run whose execution `failed`, or any check the datasource threw on (`error`) → **Errors**; a `skip` (a precondition wasn't met — the batch hasn't landed) → **Degraded**; a clean concluded run → **Reachable**; nothing run → **No runs**.
- **Data-quality health** — the ADR 0005 severity-weighted verdict of the suites. Operational `error`/`skip` results never rank here (#122 semantics).

**Derivation (documented, no new polling loop):** both read straight off the runs already recorded. New `run_service.operational_result_flags` is the exact complement of `check_outcome_counts` (which deliberately drops `skip`/`error`); those dropped rows *are* the signal that DataQ couldn't evaluate. They roll up into two new `AssetSummary` fields, `has_operational_error` + `has_skip`.

## Two bugs this surfaced and fixes
- Suite health now keys **"Passing" off `checks_total > 0`** (evaluated checks), not "a run happened" — so a run that evaluated nothing reports **No data**, not a green Passing it hasn't earned.
- The dashboard needs-attention rule moves to `has_operational_error`, which also catches a run that **succeeded while its checks errored** — the old run-status-only rule read that as healthy.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Two clearly-labelled health indicators on AssetDetail (+ tooltips); lead-strip attention rule updated
- [x] Connection-health derivation documented (service/schema/health.ts docstrings + docs/features.md); **no new polling loop**
- [x] Operational `error`/`skip` results feed connection health, not suite health (#122)

## Testing
- Backend: 5 new service tests pinning the separation — a *succeeded* run whose check `error`ed (connection Errors, suite untouched, the case `has_failed_run` alone misses), `skip` = degraded-not-error, failed run = operational error, and the mirror case (bad data must **not** dirty connection health). Full suite 2092 passed, 98.28%.
- Frontend: 11 new `connectionHealth`/`suiteHealth` unit tests (incl. the "No data, never green" invariant) + 2 AssetDetail tests. Full suite 514 passed.
- Full local gate green: black, ruff, mypy, eslint, prettier, tsc. Verified live against the local stack (screenshot above).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

result.output: Launching skill: code-review

content.text:
`high effort → 8 inline angles → dedup (no verify) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** in sequence yourself, in THIS context — do NOT spawn subagents for them. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates are the dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Dedup only (no verify)

Pool all candidates. Dedup near-duplicates only (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). Do NOT run verifiers; do NOT re-judge. Sort by severity.

## Output

Target **at least 5 findings**. If fewer genuine findings exist, emit what you have — do not invent to hit the floor.

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.

+def operational_result_flags(
+    session: Session, run_ids: Sequence[uuid.UUID]
+) -> dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[bool, bool]]:
+    """Per-run ``(has_error, has_skip)`` over its **operational** results (#122).
+
+    The exact complement of :func:`check_outcome_counts`, which counts only the
+    *evaluated* severity tiers and deliberately drops ``skip``/``error``. Those
+    dropped rows are the signal that DataQ could not evaluate a check — the
+    datasource threw (``error``) or a precondition wasn't met (``skip``) — so the
+    asset view reads them to derive **connection** health (can we reach the thing?)
+    separately from **suite** health (is the data good?), per #803.
+
+    Presence, not counts: one grouped query, a row exists iff that status occurs.
+    """
+    if not run_ids:
+        return {}
+    rows = session.execute(
+        select(Result.run_id, Result.status)
+        .where(Result.run_id.in_(run_ids), Result.status.in_(RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES))
+        .group_by(Result.run_id, Result.status)
+    ).all()
+    flags: dict[uuid.UUID, tuple[bool, bool]] = {}
+    for run_id, status in rows:
+        has_error, has_skip = flags.get(run_id, (False, False))
+        flags[run_id] = (has_error or status == "error", has_skip or status == "skip")
+    return flags
+
+
 def get_run(session: Session, run_id: uuid.UUID) -> Run | None:
     """Fetch a run by id (no authz — the API layer gates on the run's suite)."""
     return session.get(Run, run_id)

+# ── connection health vs suite health (#803) ─────────────────────────────────
+#
+# The two axes must not bleed into each other: operational `error`/`skip` results
+# (#122) feed *connection* health (could DataQ reach the datasource?) and are

+def _suite_with_run(db: Any, owner: User, *, run_status: str, result_statuses: list[str]) -> Asset:
+    """A suite on a fresh asset with one run carrying `result_statuses` results."""
+    conn = _conn(db, owner)
+    suite = suite_service.create_suite(
+        db,
+        name=f"S-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
+        description=None,

+    )
+    run = Run(suite_id=suite.id, status=run_status, triggered_by="manual")
+    db.add(run)
+    db.flush()
+    for status in result_statuses:
+        check = Check(
+            suite_id=suite.id,
+            name=f"c-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6]}",
+            expectation_type="expect_column_to_exist",
+            config={"column": "X"},
+        )
+        db.add(check)
+        db.flush()
+        db.add(Result(run_id=run.id, check_id=check.id, status=status))

+    return db.get(Asset, suite.asset_id)
+
+
+def test_error_result_feeds_connection_health_not_suite_health(db_session: Any) -> None:

+    owner = _user(db_session)

+    s = svc.summarize_asset(db_session, asset, user_id=owner.id, include_all=True)

+    assert s.has_failed_run is False  # the run itself succeeded
+    assert s.worst_severity is None  # suite axis: no DQ verdict at all
+    assert s.checks_total == 0  # `error` is not an evaluated check

+
